data.title: Economic Uncertainty And Stagflation Risks Higher, According To Fed
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Federal Reserve issued a statement where it kept the target of the federal funds rate at 4-1/4 to 4-1/2 percent. While nobody was surprised by the Fed staying put on rates, the markets got a little jittery, with the Fed stating that “uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further” and “the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.” While not mentioning trade or tariffs in the policy statement, the Fed is clearly concerned, and I am hoping that they do not act prematurely to the economic data.

Where We Currently Stand

The Federal Reserve has a dual mandate, to keep inflation at 2 percent while maintaining full employment. Over the past few years, inflation has risen far above the committee’s target, and it was done in part thanks to an overheating labor market with better than full unemployment levels.

Over the last two years, unemployment ticked up towards a more stable level and inflation declined, despite stalling over the last twelve months. Currently, unemployment is sitting right at the target full-employment level and inflation is 0.8% higher than the Fed’s long-term target.

Despite the current levels of unemployment, the Federal Reserve is still expecting the unemployment rate to rise 0.2% to 4.4% by the end of this year. In theory, the labor market should continue to soften regardless of trade, as short-term interest rates remain restrictive and long-term interest rates remain stubbornly close to 5%.

The Market Sees More Easing Ahead

The market trades Fed funds futures to project where they think interest rates over time. Back in January, the futures market had the Fed funds rate dipping to slightly below 4% by early 2026 with no real cuts being seen in the first part of next year. While rates have been higher for longer and pushed the near-term Fed funds expectations a little higher, the market now expects the Fed Funds rate to drop 100 basis points over the next twelve months, which is nearly 75 basis points below the Fed’s projections.

Looking Ahead - The Importance of June’s Meeting

Today’s meeting was not going to shine any light on the current economic situation, as the trade war is still fresh and economic data has not pointed to any developing trends. But, by June’s Fed meeting, investors may have better insight into how the economy is going to perform. By the June meeting, we should have clarification on whether trade deals will be reached or if the upcoming tariff deadlines will be allowed to expire.

Additionally, the Fed will update its economic projections to help investors gauge where the committee thinks the economy is headed. There is only one labor market report between now and the next meeting, but a surge in jobless claims following a soft May employment number may be enough to persuade the Fed. On the inflation side, the Fed will see one more PCE report and has consistently increased its inflation expectations. I’m expecting the drop in energy and commodity prices to have a positive impact on upcoming inflation reports.

The Risk of Acting Prematurely

Since the Fed decided to decisively mention that the risks of stagflation are higher, I am obviously concerned that the Fed may act prematurely to stimulate the economy. Just like during COVID, premature monetary easing without understanding the trends in economic data carries the risk of bringing inflation back to the economy and raising interest rates to new generational highs to tap it back down. I’m hoping the Fed holds on to its current trajectory until it sees weakness.

Conclusion

Post trade war data has been scant, and what information we have shows that the economy is holding up. Investors should continue to proceed with caution and look at jobless claims, CPI, and PCE data to see if there are undercurrents eroding the state of the economy. Until then, the Fed is right to hold rates and wait for further information.

data.title: Meta: Resilient In The Face Of Uncertainty Thanks To AI
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:META) released its first-quarter results last week. In this article, I am initiating my coverage on META by analyzing its Q1 report and examining what impact, if any, the recent macroeconomic uncertainties have had on the business. I also analyze the recent announcements made by the company on the AI front, and what impact they could have on the future growth.

A Snapshot of Q1 Earnings Report

It was a strong quarterly performance from META in my opinion. More specifically, Q1 revenues came in at $42.3 billion, up 16.1% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $952.6 million. Diluted EPS of $6.43 was up 36.5% y/y and comfortably beat analyst estimates by $1.22. The company also generated strong operating margins, which came in at 41%, representing an expansion of 300 bps (or 3%). While daily active users showed a 6% y/y growth, average revenue per person grew 10% y/y, despite a significant decline in Europe. Finally, the company generated $10.3 billion in free cash flows and ended the quarter with $28.75 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

Management’s guidance was also very strong. More specifically, Q2 revenues are now expected to come in between $42.5 billion and $45.5 billion, significantly higher than the company’s prior guidance of a range between $39.5 billion and $41.8 billion. Management expects full-year 2025 expenses to decrease to a range between $113 billion and $118 billion.

There were no signs of a slowdown in AI spending, as many on Wall Street had feared. On the contrary, citing growing demand, management increased the 2025 capex guidance and now project capital expenditures for the full year to be in the range between $64 billion and $72 billion. This is a significant increase from the previous guidance of a range between $60 billion and $65 billion. Finally, the tax rates for 2025 are now estimated to be between 12% and 15%.

Increase in Capex Spend Justified as AI Already Paying Off for Meta

META’s announcement that it would boost its capex investments for the year did come as a surprise to me, especially given the challenging macroenvironment that the company operates in. However, when one looks at the company’s ads business and user engagement, then it becomes very clear that its AI investments are already paying off, especially in the U.S.

Take its advertising business for instance. Unlike its peers, the company’s ad business has been resilient so far. And it has its AI investments to thank for this resilience. As mentioned earlier, the company’s average revenue per person jumped nearly 10% y/y in Q1 and while the y/y growth has slowed overall, when one looks at the U.S. & Canada market, then it shows that the growth has picked up once again, after an initial decline.

When one looks at the management commentary, then it becomes clear that AI has played a major role in ensuring that the company’s ad revenues have remained resilient, even when times were tough. For instance, in Q1, the company saw a 30% jump in the number of advertisers using AI creative tools. Moreover, the company tested a new ads recommendation model for reels, which saw conversion rates grow by 5%.

Finally, AI is driving up the rate of engagement, as seen by the 7%, 6%, and 35% jump in engagement on Facebook, Instagram, and Threads respectively. What is interesting to me is by leveraging AI, the company has managed to turbocharge the user engagement in Threads, as evidenced by the fact that it now has 350 million monthly active users.

In addition to ads, the company’s hardware business has also received a boost via AI. Ray-Ban Meta AI Glasses for instance, have seen its sales triple. More recently, the company tightened its privacy policy change in order to improve the Ray-Ban Meta experience, and while this can raise privacy issues, it should allow the company to train its AI models used on the glasses faster, which should improve the overall experience, including enhancing the model’s ability to process “different accents, dialects, and patterns of speech,” (users can delete each recording from the Ray-Ban Meta companion app if they don’t want the device to use their data).

Meta’s Reality Labs have been losing money on a consistent basis for a while now. While in the short-term, I do expect the division to continue to lose money, in the long-run, I anticipate the Ray-Ban Meta AI Glasses to be the catalyst that would unlock profitability and growth for the division, especially since the overall segment is expected to grow at over 60% CAGR through 2029.

Finally, there’s the recently launched standalone Meta AI app, expected to compete with ChatGPT and other agents. While the agentic AI space is getting crowded, the natural question is whether there was any need for Meta to launch a standalone app, especially since Meta AI is available across its family of apps. This question was asked during the Q1 earnings call and CEO Mark Zuckerberg suggested that the standalone app could be important in the U.S. given that WhatsApp is playing second fiddle to iMessage in the region. As such, a standalone app, especially in the U.S., would allow the company to differentiate itself, given it has an added social media component, which other AI-bots don’t have at present.

Taking all these use cases together, it is clear that AI has played a major role in not only ensuring that the company’s core business remains intact even during challenging times, unlike its peers, but also, it has allowed the company to build its next growth catalysts. Moreover, given that the company is already generating an ROI on its investments and given that the demand for its AI tools is expected to surge, both from an internal and external perspective, the increase in capex spending makes complete sense in my opinion. With regulatory overhangs and a challenging macroenvironment looming over its head, it is vital that the company continues to leverage its AI capabilities to maintain its growth story, even if it means spending more money than before.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$703

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

23.5x

Projected FY25 EPS

$26.69

Projected Earnings Growth

11.88%

Projected FY26 EPS

$29.86

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv) and Author’s Calculations.

According to LSEG Data, the consensus FY25 EPS estimate for META is $25.39. On average, the company has beaten analyst estimates in three of the last five years, with FY21 and FY22 being the exceptions. The average magnitude of the beat in those three years was 5.33%. Assuming the company beats the FY25 estimate by this margin, not unreasonable in my opinion given the strong start it has had to the year, then the FY25 projected EPS would be $26.74. This would represent a y/y growth of 12.1%, marginally higher than its long-term average growth of 11.65%.

According to LSEG Data, META currently trades at a forward P/E of 22.1x, making it the second cheapest name amongst the Sensational Six, with only Alphabet (GOOG) having a lower multiple. However, it is currently trading in line with its historical multiple (5-year historical median P/E multiple is 22.5x). The median P/E of the Sensational Six currently stands at 25x.

The table below shows the price targets under four different combinations of forward P/E multiples and EPS growth rates.

Based on the above table, the average price target for META comes to $703, which represents and upside of about 20% from current levels. The company also has decent momentum, as reflected by the B+ grade on the Seeking Alpha Momentum Scale. However, short-term momentum is relatively weak with a grade of C. Given the weakness in short-term momentum, despite the significant upside, my rating on the stock is a Buy rather than a Strong Buy.

Risk Factors

The biggest risk factor to my bull case is the regulatory troubles currently plaguing the company. Management, during the earnings call, already hinted that there would be a potential headwind on revenues from Europe as a result of the Digital Markets Act. While the company has said that it would appeal, management mentioned that the headwinds would appear nonetheless. Then there’s the massive antitrust trial, which is currently ongoing, through which, the FTC is seeking to break up Meta. It would be a massive blow to the company should it lose, although the final outcome could be a long time away.

The other risk factor to my bull case is the macro headwinds to its ads business. Management, during the earnings call, already cited that “Asia based e-commerce exporters,” have already reduced their spending ahead of the de minimis exemption. The average revenue per ad has also seen a steep decline in Europe. And should conditions worsen in the U.S., it would have a negative impact on the company’s top- and bottom-lines.

Concluding Thoughts

META had a solid start to the year with a beat on both the top- and bottom-lines. Operating margins also came in strong and it generated strong free cash flows. At the same time, the company announced that it would be significantly boosting its capex spend to keep up with the AI demand.

To me, the increased spending makes sense since the company is already reaping the rewards from its AI investments. The ads business has been resilient, the Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses are flying off the shelves, and the standalone Meta AI app is well-positioned to grow even in a crowded space. Moreover, Threads is on track to be its next core app, thanks to a surge in engagement, once again thanks to its AI models.

From a valuation perspective, META remains attractive at current levels in my opinion. However, the short-term momentum of the stock is weak, so a dollar cost averaging strategy would be my preferred way of getting into the name. Investors have always questioned whether the investments made by the likes of MSFT and AMZN would pay off within a reasonable timeline. When it comes to META though, in my opinion, investors should have no such qualms.

data.title: Materialise: Medical Segment Continues To Thrive
data.description:
While Materialise's (NASDAQ:MTLS) Q1 results were a mixed bag, they were well received by investors due to extremely low expectations. The Medical segment continues to grow at a healthy pace, and Materialise's guidance suggests that tariffs will be less of a headwind than initially feared. If Materialise can gain exposure to the defense sector, there could also be tailwinds in Europe.

I previously suggested that manufacturing headwinds were a distraction. Materialise's value is more dependent on its Medical segment, which continues to grow at a healthy pace, and its Software segment, which is steadily transitioning to a subscription model, despite recent revenue weakness. I continue to believe that Materialise will need to stabilize its Manufacturing and Software business and begin improving its margins before the stock moves meaningfully higher though.

Market Conditions

While Materialise has faced a challenging demand environment for some time, increased uncertainty appears to be placing further pressure on its core markets at the moment. In addition to structural issues facing European automotive manufacturers, the imposition of tariffs in the US could impact the global goods economy. Materialise's direct exposure to tariffs isn't that large though, as it has a manufacturing plant in the US that supports its healthcare business and its Software business will not be affected. The company's Manufacturing business is also focused on Europe.

Materialise expects raw material costs to be impacted though, and there is also the likelihood of manufacturing activity deteriorating, leading to less demand for Materialise's services. Manufacturing survey data in the US is currently pointing to this, although the ultimate severity is unclear at this stage.

On a positive note, manufacturing activity in Europe could be poised to rebound somewhat on the back of lower energy costs, increased fiscal stimulus and a desire for greater sovereign defense capabilities. Materialise doesn't appear to have material exposure to the defense sector at this stage though.

Materialise Business Updates

Materialise's Medical segment is being driven by a combination of solid software growth and the adoption of personalized solutions in areas like orthopedics, CMF, cardiac and respiratory. Materialise continues to improve its segmentation, planning and design software to drive further adoption. This includes the introduction of Mimics Flow, an integrated cloud solution which utilizes AI to improve segmentation and planning and enables real-time file sharing. While this software was only introduced in late 2024, customers are reportedly already seeing efficiency gains. Materialise has also introduced segmentation and planning workflows that have been designed for specific applications, like CMF.

In addition, Materialise is focused on expanding its portfolio of medical solutions. The company recently commenced a clinical trial in partnership with the University of Michigan for a 3D-printed, bioresorbable tracheal splint, which is aimed at infants with tracheobronchomalacia. This is a serious condition where the airway can collapse and often requires the use of a ventilator. The splint could help to reduce the burden of the condition until children outgrow it, typically at around the age of three. This will be an 8 year study involving at least 35 infants. If the trial is successful, it could also open the door for other soft tissue applications.

Within its Software segment, Materialise continues to transition to a subscription based business model. The company is also trying to introduce capabilities that help users overcome barriers to broader adoption of additive manufacturing. Materialise continues to iterate on its Magics software, including introducing capabilities that allow users to print more complex parts. The company has also developed two new build processors in partnership with Raplas and One Click Metal. Raplas is an additive manufacturing hardware provider focused on SLA and DLP for production environments. The Raplas build processor has reportedly increased printing speeds by up to 30-40%. One Click Metal offers metal 3D printing systems that are focused on ease of use and affordability. The partnership with One Click Metal is designed to increase Materialise's mid-market exposure.

The performance of Materialise's Software segment has been mixed in recent years. While the transition to recurring revenue is steadily progressing, revenue growth has been weak. Given the current macro environment, I expect printer hardware sales to come under further pressure, undermining Materialise's software revenue.

Materialise's Manufacturing segment also continues to struggle, despite revenue rebounding somewhat in the first quarter. Weakness in Q4 was driven in large part by the European automotive market. With European manufacturers facing market share losses in China and tariffs in the US, this situation could continue to deteriorate. Tariffs could also cause Chinese OEMs to increase their focus on Europe, adding to headwinds.

Materialise is assessing its ability to expand into the defense sector, which could help to offset manufacturing weakness. Given recent political development, Europe appears set to significantly increase its defense investments, with a focus on local capabilities. It will likely take time for Materialise to build a presence in defense though, and it is also not guaranteed that it can gain a foothold in this market. Its success in aerospace is a positive indicator, though.

Financial Analysis

Materialise generated 66.4 million EUR revenue in the first quarter. The Medical segment continues to drive growth, while the Software and Manufacturing segments were impacted by macro uncertainty. Medical software revenue increased 14% YoY, while device and services revenue was up 21%. The decline in Software revenue was also partially driven by an increase in revenue deferral. Macro conditions weighed on Materialise's Manufacturing business. Aerospace was a bright spot though, with sales to that segment increasing 23% YoY. Medtech is another area of strength, but automotive remains a significant headwind.

Materialise continues to expect 270-285 million EUR revenue for the full year, an increase of 4% at the midpoint. This guidance is fairly positive given the recent imposition of tariffs and the fact that foreign exchange is a headwind at the moment. Materialise does expect the second quarter to be more challenging though.

While Materialise's Software revenue continues to be disappointing, deferred revenue related to Software maintenance and license fees is increasing at a solid pace, indicating that Materialise's software business remains healthy. 80% of Materialise's Software revenue is now recurring, up from 75% in the prior quarter.

Materialise's operating expenses increased roughly 7% YoY in Q1, driven by R&D in support of the Medical segment. First quarter EBIT totaled 0.6 million EUR, while cash flow from operations was nearly 10 million EUR. Capital expenditures totaled 1.8 million EUR, declining to a more normal level now that most of the investments related to the new ACTech plant have been completed.

While Materialise expects margins to come under pressure in Q2, I expect margins to begin trending higher going forward. For example, Materialise incurred a number of expenses in 2024 that won't be repeated (integration of FEops, restructuring costs and the ACTech plant start-up). Growth of the Medical segment will also continue to be a structural profitability tailwind and Software margins should recover once the transition to recurring revenue is completed.

Conclusion

The decline of Materialise's share price in recent years is understandable given the post-COVID bubble and the struggles of the business. This has gone too far over the past 2 years though. Materialise is likely to continue generating high single digit revenue growth long-term and should generate healthy double-digit margins once it moves past current headwinds.

Materialise's market capitalization is only around 2.3x its Medical segment revenue, a business that is highly profitable and has grown at a 14% CAGR over the past 5 years. The company also has a net cash position of almost 68 million EUR. This probably doesn't matter while Materialise's margins and revenue remain under pressure from manufacturing weakness. The current situation will not persist indefinitely though, and in the mean time, Materialise's Medical segment will continue to grow in relative importance.

data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals: Q1 Earnings Showcase Growing Momentum
data.description:
As an analyst tracking small-cap biotech names, Rigel Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:RIGL) has always been one of my favorites, thanks to the company’s evolution from a TAVALISSE-centric company to a hematologic biotech that has multiple commercial products. It has been roughly seven months since my last Rigel article, where I reviewed the company’s Q2 2024 earnings and discussed the company’s growth opportunities and how that could have impacted the ticker’s valuation. Since then, the ticker is up over 20% and the company is showing absurd momentum on all fronts, which was showcased in their Q1 earnings, with a strong beat on EPS and revenue.

Rigel looks to be on track to hit or exceed the high end of their $200M–$210M 2025 annual revenue guidance, suggesting the ticker might be ready to transition from a speculative biotech into a more durable, execution-focused growth stock worthy of a spot in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

I intend to review the company’s Q1 earnings highlights, including the revenue acceleration from their three commercial products. In addition, I will go over the company’s profitability metrics, pipeline progress, and IP wins. Then, I will point out a few downside risks that investors should make note of when managing their RIGL position. Finally, I take a look at the charts to see if I can identify an opportunity to manage my RIGL.

Revenue Acceleration

Rigel posted total Q1 revenues of $53.3M, which were up 68% year-over-year. I must say that the level of top-line acceleration is remarkable, but it is important to underline that $43.6M of it came from net product sales, with the rest coming from collaboration and license-related revenues. This is significant because it tells me that Rigel is no longer depending on upfront and milestone payments from partners, but is a durable commercial biotech.

Perhaps the most impressive item to highlight is that TAVALISSE brought home $28.5M in Q1, which was a 35% year-over-year increase. TAVALISSE has been the company’s workhorse for a few years now, so it is encouraging to see both organic demand growth and strong execution at this point in the drug’s commercial lifespan.

Despite the RET inhibitor market being very competitive, GAVRETO has already pulled in $9M in Q1. Meanwhile, REZLIDHIA delivered $6.1M, which is up 25% year-over-year. That level of growth from these drugs points to growing confidence from prescribers and that Rigel is establishing a beachhead in hematology centers.

Rigel’s triad of products is providing them with a commercial base that’s diverse enough to hedge product-specific risk, yet still focused enough to allow them to scale efficiently. One could say that Rigel is operating in a Goldilocks zone that allows them to accelerate their product revenues, without a dramatic increase in expenses, which is ideal for a small-cap commercial-stage biotech.

However, I must also point out that the company still has significant partnership revenue coming. Rigel reported $4.7M from Grifols S.A. (GRFS), with an additional $4.6M coming from Kissei Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:KSPHF ) (TYO: 4547). In addition, Medison Pharma sent Rigel $400K for product deliveries and earned royalties. So, although Rigel is not solely relying on partnership and contract revenue, pulling in nearly $10M in Q1 can be a nice sweetener from time to time.

Proof is in the Profitability

Not only did Rigel have impressive revenue marks, but the company also revealed that their Q1 net income was $11.4M, compared to an $8.2M loss in Q1 of last year. What is more, Rigel’s gross margins improved, while expenses were fairly consistent year-over-year; there was a slight uptick in R&D costs to support their pipeline efforts.

To me, this shows that they are demonstrating operating leverage, where revenue growth is outpacing the costs. Not only does this signal a maturation out of the speculative label, but also gives the company some additional freedom to invest more in their R&D, pipeline, or possibly look for another acquisition… without the need for dilutive fundraising.

Pipeline Progress

Taking a look at Rigel’s pipeline, the company’s lead pipeline program is R289, an IRAK1/4 inhibitor being pitted against relapsed/refractory lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS).

Rigel has completed the dose escalation portion of R289’s Phase Ib study and initiated dosing in the expansion phase. The company also communicated with the FDA about a potential registrational path and plans to present their dose escalation data at a conference in 2H of this year. This program has already secured both Orphan Drug and Fast Track status from the FDA, so the regulatory path is shortened and accelerated.

If the data holds up, R289 might be a major player in the MDS arena and could be entering the market with a first-in-class or best-in-class due to its oral formulation. Roughly 12.2K lower-risk MDS patients have been previously treated, so there’s a significant commercial opportunity that could attract suitors looking for a licensing or partnership with Rigel.

Clarity on TAVALISSE

One important item that didn’t appear to make any big headlines, was the TAVALISSE patent settlement with Annora Pharma. The settlement push a potential generic entry to no earlier than Q2 2032, so Rigel currently has a seven-year runway.

Not only does that ruling allow Rigel to maintain its position in the market, but it also gives the visibility needed to predict cash flows and reduce the number of uncertainties. Not only does this increase the value of TAVALISSE, but extending the lifecycle of their flagship product could give Rigel enough time to start developing another drug or acquire a replacement product.

Remnant Risks

Although I just went over some bullish highlights from the company’s Q1 earnings, I must point out that risks remain for Rigel. Despite the recent commercial success of their three product, the company is still heavily dependent on TAVALISSE, GAVRETO, and REZLIDHIA revenue to stay afloat. As a result, a disruption in one of those products could generate an abrupt change in the company’s outlook. Keep in mind, the competition in both the RET and IDH1-mutant AML markets is incredibly intense, so investors shouldn’t just chalk up growth for GAVRETO and REZLIDHIA for the next five years.

Investors should also keep an eye on R&D spending as it continues to climb in order to maintain their clinical efforts. Although R289 appears to be a promising drug, investment in the program will be costly as we move deeper into development. Therefore, Rigel must maintain their profitability to avoid being forced to perform dilutive financing to maintain their pipeline programs, commercial efforts, and keep the lights on. Reminder, while the Annora settlement removes near-term patent risk on TAVALISSE until 2032, we can also say there is now a countdown for Rigel to either find a replacement or amass enough new revenue streams before the loss of exclusivity of their flagship product.

Essentially, all the positive items I highlighted in this earnings review still have remnant risk. Although the company has an ample amount of momentum, investors need to see that it is a delicate balance that needs to be maintained if Rigel is to become a reliable growth stock. I must point out that the company only has ~$77.1M in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, so the company cannot afford for more than one thing to fall out of place before legitimate fears of dilution begin to rise.

Despite these risks, I am maintaining my RIGL conviction level at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio.

Looking for a Buy

The market’s initial reaction to Rigel’s Q1 earnings was very positive. However, the rally was short-lived, as the share price found resistance right on a trendline at $25.42 per share, the day after the earnings report, and the ticker sold off going into the close. Now, the ticker is trading at $18.25 per share, which is below my current RIGL Buy Threshold of $20.45.

As a result, I am searching for a potential buy if the ticker can break and hold above that $17.30 resistance level. Subsequently, I will look to make another addition if the ticker can hold above the 200-Day EMA around $17.75 per share. Admittedly, I wouldn’t be making a huge addition because the technicals aren’t incredibly bullish, but I am comfortable adding based on the company’s growth and momentum.

Once I have added to the position, I will add on to some of my sell orders around my Sell Targets at $40.10 and $49 per share in order to book profit and move the RIGL position back to a “House Money” position. Ultimately, my plan is to repeat this process a least one more time in order to generate profits while growing a core position to transfer to my RIGL position to my “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.
